name: Iran
tactical_document:
# Iran — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Amir Ghalenoei's Iran is the physical, set-piece-laden, counter-attacking heavyweight of Asia — the antithesis of a possession game. Where elite sides want 65% of the ball, Iran is happy with 40% if they can run hard, foul cleverly, win every aerial duel, and bury one Mehdi Taremi half-chance per match. The identity is **physicality, organization, and clinical counter-attacks** built around captain and lone striker **Mehdi Taremi**, supported by the wide creativity of veteran Alireza Jahanbakhsh and the technical control of Saman Ghoddos. Iran's defensive culture is unmatched in the region: they will defend a 1-0 lead with eleven men behind the ball for 70 minutes and never panic. Recent form: qualified comfortably and early from AFC qualifying, regularly the toughest matchup for Japan and South Korea, with the 2022 Wales win still proving they can beat second-tier European sides at a World Cup. For 2026 (their seventh World Cup, drawn in Group G with Belgium, Egypt and New Zealand), Ghalenoei built a domestic-heavy, defensively disciplined squad. **Sardar Azmoun was controversially omitted** from the final 26, leaving Taremi as the undisputed focal point of the attack. **Matchday 1 (vs New Zealand, June 15) finished 2-2** — Iran twice took the lead (Rezaeian 32', Mohebi 64') but were pegged back by an Elijah Just brace, a defensive lapse leaving them on one point. **Matchday 2 (vs Belgium, June 21) finished 0-0** — a textbook Iranian display: a compact low block, every aerial duel contested, Beiranvand untroubled, Taremi the lone outlet, and Iran content to grind a goalless point off a 10-man Belgium. That clean sheet pushed Iran to **2 points** and set up a winner-takes-most finale. **Matchday 3 is Egypt v Iran, Friday June 26, Lumen Field, Seattle.** Group G after MD2: **Egypt 4, Iran 2, Belgium 2, New Zealand 1** (all five games so far have featured draws among the top three). Crucially, **Iran need only a DRAW vs Egypt to reach the knockout stage for the first time in their history** — and that is exactly the kind of match Iran is built to manage. A win would top the group; a loss likely sends them out. Ghoddos recovered from the MD1 ankle knock and started MD2 — he is fully fit. The one warning sign: **Ezatolahi picked up a yellow card in MD2 and is now one booking from a one-match ban**, so the destroyer must pick his tactical fouls with care in a knockout-defining game.

## Formation
- Shape: **4-2-3-1** in possession (collapses to a **4-5-1 / 4-2-3-1 low block** out of possession; Ghalenoei has a "plan B" **3-6-1 / 5-4-1** he uses against elite opponents like Belgium). The double pivot screens the back four; Taremi is the lone striker with a three-man band behind him.
- Role mapping (roster order in `iran.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — **Alireza Beiranvand** (#1) — physically dominant (strength 16), long-throw weapon (famous for hurling the ball into the opposition box), traditional shot-stopper rather than a sweeper. First-choice for a third consecutive World Cup.
  - index 1: LB — **Milad Mohammadi** (#5) — veteran left-back, positionally disciplined, rarely overlaps deep, underlaps to support the cutting-inside winger.
  - index 2: LCB — **Shojae Khalilzadeh** (#4) — the aerial monster (strength 17), the duel-winner, the focal point of set-piece defending; the one centre-back guaranteed his place.
  - index 3: RCB — **Hossein Kanaanizadegan** (#8) — physical, experienced stopper (strength 16), the organizer who wins the duels alongside Khalilzadeh.
  - index 4: RB — **Saleh Hardani** (#13) — energetic, athletic right-back (speed 15, stamina 17), gets forward on the right when Jahanbakhsh tucks inside.
  - index 5: DM (pivot) — **Saeid Ezatolahi** (#6) — the destroyer, the screen, the tempo-controller and tactical fouler who breaks up every opposition attack 30 yards from goal. The defensive heartbeat of the double pivot.
  - index 6: DM (pivot) — **Saman Ghoddos** (#14) — the deeper-lying creator of the pivot pair; the most technical midfielder (pass 15), drops alongside Ezatolahi to build, then sprays the diagonals that launch the counters.
  - index 7: LW / left of the three — **Mohammad Mohebi** (#11) — pacy, direct wide-left runner (speed 16, dribbling 15), the counter-attack outlet who beats his man and gets to the byline.
  - index 8: CAM / #10 — **Mehdi Ghayedi** (#10) — the central attacking midfielder behind Taremi, a dribble-first second-striker type (dribbling 16) who runs in behind and links the pivots to the striker.
  - index 9: RW / right of the three — **Alireza Jahanbakhsh** (#7) — experienced winger and dead-ball specialist, cuts inside on his left foot; long-serving senior leader (vice-captain) and chief creator.
  - index 10: CF — **Mehdi Taremi** (#9) — **captain**, talisman and lone striker; drops into the #10 space to receive between the lines, makes runs in behind to get 1v1 with the keeper, the team's leading scorer and clinical finisher. Strength 16, shoot 17, offensive 17.

## Style of Play

### Build-up
Direct and pragmatic. Iran does NOT play out from the back when pressed — Beiranvand will launch a long ball to Taremi or into the channel for Mohebi/Jahanbakhsh to chase. When time permits, Khalilzadeh plays it short to Kanaanizadegan or out to Mohammadi, who passes to Ezatolahi, who recycles to Ghoddos. The first instinct is always **find Taremi between the lines** — he is the pivot. The team is comfortable being patient when not pressed, but the moment a press arrives, the ball goes long. Beiranvand's long throw is a weapon — when Iran wins a throw-in inside the opposition half, Beiranvand sprints up to launch it into the box.

### Pressing
**Mid-block, selective triggers.** Iran does not press the opposition GK. They drop into the **4-5-1** at the halfway line and wait for the ball to enter midfield. Ezatolahi steps to the opposition pivot; Ghoddos and Ghayedi jump the inside-midfielders; Taremi cover-shadows the deepest CB to deny the back-pass. Once the ball enters the wide channels, the fullback (Mohammadi or Hardani) and winger (Mohebi or Jahanbakhsh) double-team. The press is not high; it is mid-block compression.

### Defensive shape
Compact **4-5-1** with Taremi as the lone front presser, Mohebi and Jahanbakhsh dropping wide into a flat midfield five alongside the double pivot Ezatolahi and Ghoddos, with Ghayedi tucking in centrally. The back four sits deep — 25-30 units off goal — denying space in behind. The CBs (especially Khalilzadeh) win every aerial duel inside the box. Against elite opponents, Iran will shift to **5-4-1** with Hardani becoming a third CB. The shape is famously stable: hard to break down, low-line, organized.

### Wide play
Both wingers cut inside, which means the fullbacks are the natural width-givers. Mohammadi underlaps on the left; Hardani overlaps on the right. Iran's crosses come from **deep** (35-40 yards from goal) and are aimed at Taremi at the near post. Mohebi's pace down the left is the chief counter-attack outlet — Ghoddos's diagonals find him every time.

### Final third
Patterns: Taremi drops to receive between the lines, lays off to Ghayedi (the #10) who slips Mohebi sprinting in behind; Jahanbakhsh's cut-inside curler from the right half-space; Taremi's hold-up play and lay-off to a late-arriving Ghayedi from the #10 slot; deep cross from Hardani to Taremi at the near post. Iran is a **clinical** team — they create 5-6 chances per match and need to convert one. Taremi's shoot rating of 17 is the difference.

## Set Pieces
**Iran is one of the most dangerous set-piece teams in Asia.** Beiranvand's long throw is a weapon used 5-8 times per match. Khalilzadeh wins every header.
- Attacking corners: **Jahanbakhsh** in-swingers from the right, **Ghoddos** out-swingers from the left. Targets: Khalilzadeh (near post), Kanaanizadegan (penalty spot), Taremi (back post), lurkers at the far zone.
- Defending corners: heavy man-marking. Khalilzadeh on the most dangerous striker; Beiranvand attacks any cross within his reach.
- Free kicks: Jahanbakhsh direct from any angle within 28 yards; Taremi from central range.
- Long throws: **Beiranvand** sprints up from the goal to launch throws into the box on any throw-in inside the opposition half.
- Penalties: **Taremi** primary, **Jahanbakhsh** secondary.

## decide() Decision Priorities
1. **If my role == "GK" (index 0, Beiranvand, #1) and the team wins a throw-in inside the opposition half:** Sprint forward to within 35 units of the throw-in spot to launch a long throw into the box.
2. **If my player_id ends with "_10" (CF Taremi, #9) and team_phase == "attacking":** Drop into the #10 space between the opposition lines, 10-15 units behind the back line; turn and either dribble or Pass forward to the index-7 Mohebi sprinting in behind, or lay off to the index-8 Ghayedi arriving centrally.
3. **If my player_id ends with "_10" (CF Taremi, #9) and the ball is within 25 units of goal:** Shoot if angle < 35°; shoot rating is 17, take the chance.
4. **If my role == "DEF" and team_phase == "defending":** Stay deep — back four holds a line 25 units off goal. Do not push higher unless ball is past the halfway line.
5. **If my player_id ends with "_5" (DM Ezatolahi, #6) and the opposition has the ball within 35 units of my goal:** Step to the ball-carrier, tackle if within 4 units. Foul tactically ONLY when the opposition is genuinely breaking past me into a dangerous central lane — Ezatolahi is on a yellow and a second booking means a knockout-game suspension, so prefer a clean tackle or a delay over a needless cynical foul. The index-6 Ghoddos covers the screen behind.
6. **If team_phase == "transition_attack":** The index-7 Mohebi (LW) and index-9 Jahanbakhsh (RW) sprint forward immediately; index-10 Taremi stays central; index-8 Ghayedi runs in behind through the middle; index-6 Ghoddos delivers the long diagonal; index-5 Ezatolahi holds.
7. **If my player_id ends with "_7" (LW Mohebi, #11) and team_phase == "transition_attack":** Sprint into the channel beyond the opposition RB; receive the index-6 Ghoddos's diagonal.
8. **If my player_id ends with "_2" (LCB Khalilzadeh, #4) and a cross is incoming into my box:** Attack the ball aggressively — win the header. Strength 17.
9. **If team_phase == "defending" and the ball is wide:** Fullback + wide attacker on that side double-team the opposition wide attacker.
10. **If team is leading by 1+ goals after minute 70:** Drop into **5-4-1** with the index-4 Hardani becoming a third CB; recycle every set-piece by taking 30 seconds.
11. **If a defensive corner is incoming:** The index-2 Khalilzadeh marks the most dangerous opposition CF; the index-3 Kanaanizadegan covers the penalty spot zone; the index-0 Beiranvand attacks the cross.
12. **Set-pieces 18-30 yards from goal:** Defer to the index-9 Jahanbakhsh (right-side in-swingers) or the index-10 Taremi (central).

## Key Player Notes
- **Taremi (index 10, #9):** Captain, talisman and lone striker, false-9-meets-#10. Drops between the lines to receive, turns, and either finishes or slips through-balls. Runs in behind to get 1v1 with the keeper. The attack is built around him; primary penalty taker.
- **Jahanbakhsh (index 9, #7):** Veteran senior leader, dead-ball specialist and right-sided creator. Cuts inside, shoots curlers. The most-capped attacker and secondary penalty/free-kick taker.
- **Khalilzadeh (index 2, #4):** The aerial monster. Wins every header in both boxes. Strength 17.
- **Ezatolahi (index 5, #6):** The destroyer and defensive half of the double pivot. Tempo-controller and tactical fouler; sets the team's defensive aggression baseline. **On a yellow card from MD2 — one more booking = a one-match ban**, so his fouling must be selective in the Egypt decider.
- **Ghoddos (index 6, #14):** The deeper, more creative pivot partner; the most technical midfielder. Sprays the diagonals that launch the counters and starts the build-up. **Recovered from the MD1 ankle knock and started MD2 vs Belgium — fully fit for Egypt.**
- **Ghayedi (index 8, #10):** The dribble-first #10; pacy second-striker type who runs beyond Taremi and carries the ball in transition (dribbling 16). One to watch after recent injury recovery.
- **Mohebi (index 7, #11):** The pacy left winger and chief counter-attack outlet (speed 16); direct, beats his man, gets to the byline.
- **Beiranvand (index 0, #1):** The long-throw weapon. Iran scores 2-3 goals per tournament from his throws.

## Tournament Mindset
Iran arrives at every World Cup with a single defined goal: **escape the group stage for the first time in their history** — and against Egypt on Matchday 3, that goal is finally within a single point's reach. Two draws (2-2 NZ, 0-0 Belgium) have left Iran on 2 points, and **a draw vs Egypt qualifies them**; this is the precise scenario Ghalenoei's side was engineered for. The mentality for Seattle is **defensive solidity first, set-pieces second, Taremi third**: sit in the 4-5-1, deny Salah and the Egyptian wide runners any space in behind, win every aerial duel, manage the game's tempo, and pounce on the one Taremi half-chance or Beiranvand long throw. They will happily take a 0-0 to the 90th minute and progress on it — though a win would top the group. The risks are two: creativity in open play against a well-organized opponent (with Azmoun omitted, when Taremi is marked Iran can go a half without a clean chance), and **discipline management — Ezatolahi sitting on a yellow** means an ill-timed cynical foul could both gift Egypt a dangerous free-kick and cost Iran their pivot in the knockouts. Discipline is moderate (mostly 13-14) — expect 1-2 yellows per match. The temptation to chase a winner must never override the simple truth: **a clean draw is enough.**
